summary: Gruber doubles down on his characterization of Apple's personalized Siri features as vaporware, responding to Apple executives' attempts to push back on that narrative. He argues that even if working code existed internally, the 2024 WWDC keynote demo was edited in an unusually choppy way that broke Apple's own rules about demos being capturable in a single take, likely due to terrible latency. Sources within Apple's engineering group tell him most rank-and-file engineers never saw a functional build before the keynote. He's willing to stipulate working code existed but maintains it was clearly unshippable, and notes Apple's euphemistic 'in the coming year' timeline is misleadingly vague about when the feature will actually arrive.
key_points: Apple's 2024 WWDC Siri demo was edited with constant cuts away from the device, never showing a single action leading to the next — breaking Apple's own internal demo rules
Multiple trusted sources in Apple's software engineering group say they never saw an internal build with the personalized Siri feature before last year's keynote
Vaporware doesn't mean 'completely fabricated' — it means 'promised but hasn't shipped,' which is exactly what happened regardless of whether working code existed internally
Apple's 'in the coming year' phrasing is a misleading euphemism that could mean as late as December 2026, when they should just say 'next year'
The real criticism isn't that Apple lacks a chatbot strategy — it's that Apple is behind in executing its own stated strategy
money_quotes: Most rank and file engineers within Apple do not believe that feature existed in an even vaguely functional state a year ago, and the first any of them ever heard of it was when they watched the keynote with the rest of us on the first day of WWDC last year.
Whatever state it was in, it couldn't be shown in a single take.
It'd be crazy to announce the feature if they didn't believe they could ship it, and Apple's executives aren't crazy.
I think it was far worse than 'not up to Apple's high standards' — it was clearly unshippable.
From our perspective it doesn't really matter it was real, in any sense, a year ago. It's still vaporware at this point.
Vaporware doesn't mean 'completely fabricated'. It just means 'promised but hasn't shipped'.
It's very obvious that this year's WWDC keynote went back to an underpromise/overdeliver mindset. But 'in the coming year' is raising some users' hopes misleadingly.
key_insight: Regardless of whether Apple had working code internally, the personalized Siri feature remains vaporware by definition — promised, advertised, used to sell iPhones, but never shipped — and Apple's spin campaign to rewrite that narrative doesn't change the facts.
